The average menstrual cycle is 28 days, but anywhere from 21 to 35 days is considered normal.
Period blood is not 'dirty' — it's a mix of blood, uterine lining, mucus, and vaginal fluid.
The uterus sheds its lining every month if no fertilized egg implants.
Cramps (dysmenorrhea) are caused by prostaglandins — chemicals that cause the uterus to contract.
Exercise during your period can actually reduce cramp severity by releasing endorphins.
The average person loses only 2–3 tablespoons of blood during a period, despite it feeling like more.
Periods can sync with close friends or roommates over time — this is called the McClintock effect.
Estrogen peaks just before ovulation, giving many people a natural energy boost mid-cycle.
Progesterone rises after ovulation and can cause bloating, fatigue, and mood changes.
The follicular phase (day 1–14) is when your body prepares an egg for release.
The luteal phase (day 15–28) begins after ovulation and ends when your period starts.
Ovulation usually happens around day 14 of a 28-day cycle, but varies person to person.
An egg survives only 12–24 hours after ovulation if not fertilized.
Sperm can survive inside the body for up to 5 days, making the fertile window wider than you think.
PMS affects up to 75% of menstruating people at some point in their lives.
PMDD (Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder) is a severe form of PMS affecting mood significantly.
Chocolate cravings before your period are linked to magnesium deficiency — dark chocolate helps!
Iron levels drop during menstruation — eating iron-rich foods like spinach can help replenish them.
Heating pads work for cramps because heat relaxes the uterine muscles and improves blood flow.
The word 'menstruation' comes from the Latin 'mensis' meaning month, and Greek 'mene' meaning moon.
Many ancient cultures tracked cycles using the lunar calendar — the moon cycle is ~29.5 days.
Stress can delay or skip periods by disrupting the hormonal signals from the brain.
Intense exercise or very low body fat can cause periods to stop — called amenorrhea.
Endometriosis affects 1 in 10 people with periods and causes tissue to grow outside the uterus.
PCOS (Polycystic Ovary Syndrome) affects up to 10% of people and can cause irregular cycles.
Ibuprofen works for period pain because it blocks prostaglandins — take it with food.
Your sense of smell is strongest around ovulation — it's your body's way of detecting mates.
Many people report feeling more creative and social during the follicular phase.
The luteal phase often brings a desire to nest, clean, or organize — blame progesterone.
Period underwear can hold up to 2 teaspoons of blood and is fully washable and reusable.
Menstrual cups can be worn for up to 12 hours and hold more than a tampon.
Tampons were first mass-produced in the 1930s by Earle Haas.
The average person will have around 450 periods in their lifetime.
Menarche (first period) usually occurs between ages 9 and 16.
Menopause typically occurs between ages 45 and 55 when periods permanently stop.
Perimenopause can start up to 10 years before menopause with irregular cycles.
Swimming during your period is completely safe — water pressure reduces flow temporarily.
Period pain is the leading cause of school and work absence among young menstruating people.
Vitamin B6 can help reduce PMS symptoms like mood swings and bloating.
Magnesium supplements may reduce cramp severity and PMS symptoms.
Omega-3 fatty acids (found in fish, flaxseed) can help reduce inflammation and period pain.
Caffeine can worsen cramps by constricting blood vessels — try cutting back before your period.
Alcohol during your period can worsen bloating and mood swings.
Staying hydrated helps reduce bloating — drinking more water actually flushes excess water out.
The cervix changes position throughout the cycle — lower and firmer before a period.
Cervical mucus changes throughout the cycle — clear and stretchy at ovulation (like egg whites).
Basal body temperature rises slightly (0.2°C) after ovulation due to progesterone.
Tracking BBT (basal body temperature) can help confirm ovulation has occurred.
Light spotting around ovulation is normal and called 'ovulation bleeding' or mittelschmerz.
Mittelschmerz is a German word meaning 'middle pain' — referring to ovulation cramps.
The uterus is about the size of a pear and can expand to the size of a watermelon during pregnancy.
Period blood doesn't clot like regular blood because the uterus releases anticoagulants.
Large clots during periods (bigger than a quarter) may be worth mentioning to a doctor.
Heavy periods (menorrhagia) are defined as losing more than 80ml of blood per cycle.
Thyroid disorders can cause irregular, heavy, or absent periods.
Anemia from heavy periods can cause fatigue, dizziness, and shortness of breath.
Birth control pills work by preventing ovulation and thinning the uterine lining.
The hormonal IUD can make periods lighter or stop them completely.
The copper IUD is hormone-free and may make periods heavier initially.
Some people experience nausea during their period due to prostaglandins affecting the stomach.
Diarrhea or loose stools during periods are common — also caused by prostaglandins.
Back pain during periods is caused by referred pain from uterine contractions.
Period headaches or migraines can be triggered by the drop in estrogen before menstruation.
Keeping a period diary helps identify patterns in symptoms, mood, and flow.
Apps like Femora help you predict your cycle based on your unique history.
A cycle shorter than 21 days or longer than 35 days is worth discussing with a doctor.
Missing more than 3 consecutive periods (not due to pregnancy) should be evaluated.
Periods can affect sleep — progesterone is a natural sleep aid but its drop causes insomnia.
Many people sleep worse in the days before their period when progesterone falls.
Melatonin production can be disrupted by hormonal fluctuations during the cycle.
Yoga poses like child's pose and supine twist can help relieve period cramps.
Acupuncture has shown some evidence in reducing period pain severity.
Heat therapy is as effective as ibuprofen for mild to moderate period cramps in some studies.
The follicular phase is the best time for high-intensity workouts — energy is at its peak.
The luteal phase is better suited for yoga, pilates, and lower intensity movement.
Skin often breaks out before a period due to rising androgens and falling estrogen.
Some people experience clearer skin around ovulation when estrogen is highest.
Hair may feel fuller mid-cycle and thinner before your period due to hormonal shifts.
Estrogen promotes collagen production — skin can look more radiant in the follicular phase.
Period poverty affects millions worldwide — many miss school due to lack of menstrual products.
In many countries, menstrual products are still taxed as luxury items.
Scotland was the first country to make menstrual products free for all in 2020.
Many workplaces are now introducing 'period leave' policies.
Reusable menstrual products like cups and discs can last up to 10 years.
The menstrual disc sits at the cervix and can be worn during sex.
Some people experience a heightened libido around ovulation due to peak estrogen.
Orgasms can relieve period cramps by causing uterine contractions that expel blood faster.
The clitoris has over 8,000 nerve endings — more than any other part of the human body.
Endorphins released during orgasm act as natural painkillers.
Period syncing between partners or close friends is debated — some studies support it, others don't.
The gut microbiome can influence hormonal balance and affect cycle regularity.
A diet high in fiber helps regulate estrogen levels by supporting gut health.
Phytoestrogens in soy can mildly mimic estrogen — effect on cycles varies by person.
Seed cycling is a popular wellness practice — flax/pumpkin seeds in follicular phase, sesame/sunflower in luteal.
There's limited scientific evidence for seed cycling but many report improved symptoms.
Vitamin D deficiency is linked to more painful periods and irregular cycles.
Getting 15–20 minutes of sunlight daily supports vitamin D production and hormonal health.
Zinc supports progesterone production and may help with PMS symptoms.
Evening primrose oil is often used to support hormonal balance in the luteal phase.
Chasteberry (vitex) is an herbal supplement used traditionally to regulate cycles.
Always consult a doctor before starting supplements that affect hormones.
Irregular cycles can be caused by thyroid issues, PCOS, stress, or significant weight changes.
Tracking 3 or more cycles gives a much more accurate picture of your average cycle length.
The more cycle data you log, the more accurate your period predictions become.
Femora uses your unique cycle history — not a fixed 28-day assumption — to predict your period.
Confidence in predictions increases with every cycle you log.
Your fertile window is typically the 5 days before ovulation plus the day of ovulation.
Natural family planning methods require tracking multiple fertility signs for accuracy.
Ovulation predictor kits (OPKs) detect the LH surge that occurs 24–36 hours before ovulation.
LH (luteinizing hormone) triggers the release of the egg from the follicle.
FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) causes follicles in the ovary to mature each cycle.
The corpus luteum forms after ovulation and produces progesterone to support a potential pregnancy.
If no pregnancy occurs, the corpus luteum breaks down, progesterone drops, and the period begins.
HCG (the pregnancy hormone) is what home pregnancy tests detect.
HCG levels double every 48–72 hours in early pregnancy.
Implantation bleeding — light spotting 6–12 days after ovulation — can be mistaken for a period.
The first day of your period (full flow) is considered day 1 of your new cycle.
Spotting before your period starts doesn't count as day 1 — wait for full flow.
Period flow is typically heaviest on days 1–2 and lightens toward the end.
Night sweats before your period can be caused by the drop in progesterone.
Feeling colder during the luteal phase is normal — progesterone slightly raises body temperature.
The average age of first period has been decreasing over the past century, likely due to nutrition.
Girls who start periods earlier tend to have slightly longer reproductive lifespans.
Athletes and dancers often experience delayed menarche due to low body fat.
Running or cycling regularly can help regulate cycles by reducing stress and maintaining weight.
BMI extremes (very high or very low) can disrupt hormonal balance and cycle regularity.
Gut health and the estrobolome (gut bacteria that metabolize estrogen) affect cycle health.
Probiotics may support hormonal balance by improving gut health.
Reducing sugar intake can help balance insulin levels, which affects hormonal health.
Insulin resistance is closely linked to PCOS and irregular cycles.
Anti-inflammatory foods (berries, turmeric, leafy greens) can reduce period pain.
Red raspberry leaf tea is traditionally used to tone the uterus and ease cramps.
Ginger tea has anti-inflammatory properties and may help with nausea and cramps.
Chamomile tea has antispasmodic properties that may help relax uterine muscles.
Castor oil packs over the lower abdomen are a traditional remedy for period pain.
TENS machines (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation) can block period pain signals.
Period pain is a legitimate medical condition — you don't have to just 'push through it'.
Many people wait years before being diagnosed with endometriosis due to normalized pain.
Adenomyosis is when the uterine lining grows into the uterine muscle, causing heavy painful periods.
Fibroids are non-cancerous growths in the uterus that can cause heavy periods and pressure.
Polyps in the uterus can cause irregular bleeding and spotting between periods.
STIs like chlamydia can cause irregular bleeding — regular testing is important.
Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) can cause irregular periods and pelvic pain.
Keeping your period tracker updated helps you spot abnormal patterns early.
A sudden change in cycle length, flow, or pain is worth mentioning to a healthcare provider.
Period tracking is a form of body literacy — understanding your own patterns is empowering.
Your cycle is a vital sign — changes can signal overall health shifts.
The four phases of the cycle (menstrual, follicular, ovulatory, luteal) each affect mood and energy differently.
Working with your cycle — not against it — can improve productivity and wellbeing.
Many athletes now use cycle syncing to time training intensity with their hormonal phases.
The concept of cycle syncing was popularized by Alisa Vitti in her book 'Woman Code'.
There is growing research into how the menstrual cycle affects athletic performance.
Some studies show pain tolerance is highest mid-cycle when estrogen is elevated.
Memory and verbal skills may peak in the follicular phase due to rising estrogen.
Spatial skills may be slightly stronger in the luteal phase according to some research.
Hormonal fluctuations throughout the cycle are completely normal — not a weakness.
Understanding your cycle helps you advocate for yourself in medical settings.
Menstrual health is overall health — your cycle reflects what's happening in your body.
